title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption: From 1949 to 2014, the value of India’s merchandise exports as a share of GDP is distributed across six continental destination groups in a stacked area chart, with the vertical axis ranging from 0% to 18%. Exports to Asia form the base layer and show the most consistent growth, starting near 1% in 1949, dipping below 1% around 1965, then rising steadily after 1980 to reach approximately 8% by 2014. Exports to Western Europe, shown in maroon, begin near 2% in 1949, fall to under 1% by 1965, then climb gradually to about 3% by 2000 before accelerating to nearly 5% by 2014. Exports to North America, in green, remain below 1% until the late 1980s, then rise to roughly 2.5% by 2014. Exports to South America, in orange, stay mostly under 0.5% throughout the period, with minor fluctuations. Exports to Africa, in yellow, hover between 0.2% and 0.8%, peaking briefly around 1975 and again near 2008. Exports to Eastern Europe, in purple, are negligible until the early 1970s, then rise slightly to about 0.5% by 1990 before declining again. Exports to Oceania, in pink, remain consistently below 0.3% across all years. The total export share rises from about 4% in 1949 to over 16% by 2014, with the largest absolute increases occurring after 1990 and concentrated in exports to Asia and Western Europe. The dominant visible pattern is the sustained expansion of export intensity toward Asian and Western European markets, particularly from the 1990s onward, while other regions contribute smaller, more stable shares.
